data.title: tether.wallet: Tether's most important strategic transformation in 12 years
data.description:
Written by: JSAI@Jinse Finance On April 14, 2026, Tether, the world's largest stablecoin issuer, officially launched its first consumer-grade product for ordinary users—tether.wallet, positioned as "The People's Wallet." This marks the first time in twelve years that the company has moved from a purely backend infrastructure role to a user-facing one. I. What is tether.wallet? Core Functions and Asset Support tether.wallet is a self-custodied crypto wallet that allows users to hold and send USDT, USAT, XAUT, and BTC across multiple blockchains. Tether has deliberately simplified its asset support. The wallet only supports four assets: USDT (the world's most widely used stablecoin), USAT (Tether's compliant stablecoin for the US market), XAUT (gold-backed token), and BTC. There are no altcoins, no DeFi tokens, and no NFTs. Behind this "minimalist asset list" lies a clear product philosophy: a payment tool and a store of value, not a speculative platform. In terms of network support, USDT and XAUT initially support Ethereum, Polygon, Plasma, and Arbitrum; USAT only supports Ethereum; and Bitcoin supports both the mainnet and the Lightning Network. Tether stated that it will continue to expand the supported blockchain networks. Three Major Experience Innovations: Human-readable addresses, eliminating alphanumeric errors. Users can send funds using human-readable formats such as [email protected], completely eliminating the historical problem of irreversible errors caused by long alphanumeric wallet addresses. This design is similar to the logic of email addresses, bringing the cryptocurrency transfer experience closer to the cognitive comfort zone of ordinary people. Fees are paid directly with transferred assets, without the need for Gas tokens. The wallet removes common friction points: users no longer need to hold separate Gas tokens to pay transaction fees; fees are deducted directly from the transferred assets. This is significant for ordinary users—previously, someone holding only USDT needed to purchase ETH or POL as Gas fees before initiating a transfer, a barrier that deterred many non-professional users. Fully self-custodied, the keys are always in the user's hands. The wallet is fully self-custodied. Private keys and mnemonic phrases are generated and stored on the user's device; no transactions are signed on the server, and Tether never holds user funds. Furthermore, the wallet supports securely backing up private keys to the cloud, a feature that has attracted attention from some in the crypto community—critics point out that similar recovery tools previously launched by Ledger have been controversial due to cloud backups, and it is currently unclear whether Tether will allow users to completely disable cloud backups.

Underlying Technology: The Dual Engines of WDK and QVAC

tether.wallet is not an independent product, but rather a consumer portal within Tether's broader technological strategy.

WDK (Wallet Development Kit) is its technological foundation. Tether open-sourced WDK in October 2025—a modular, multi-chain self-custodial wallet building tool designed for developers, institutions, and even sovereign nations, aiming to support humans, connected devices, and AI agents in securely holding and exchanging value on open networks. WDK's first public application is Rumble Wallet, launched in January 2026, connecting crypto payments to 80 million users on the Rumble platform; tether.wallet is the flagship consumer product built on the same foundation.

QVAC is Tether's self-developed localized AI computing platform. QVAC enables wallets to run AI functions locally on user devices, enabling intelligent tasks such as transaction management and financial insights, while protecting user privacy by not uploading data to the cloud.

QVAC is Tether's self-developed localized AI computing platform. QVAC allows wallets to run AI functions locally on user devices, enabling intelligent tasks such as transaction management and financial insights, while not uploading data to the cloud.

This architecture circumvents the cloud-based data processing model commonly used by large technology platforms, aligning perfectly with the current market trend of prioritizing privacy.

II. Why is Tether launching tether.wallet now?

The answer to this question can be interpreted from three dimensions: strategy, business, and regulation.

Strategic Level: Bridging the "Last Mile," Upgrading from Infrastructure to Platform

For over twelve years, Tether has been the underlying layer of the digital asset economy—providing liquidity, settlement, and payment capabilities in over 160 countries. Users have never directly interacted with Tether products. tether.wallet changes all of that.

This is a textbook example of vertical integration. Imagine a company that owns the world's largest water pipe network but has never sold water directly to users—all end-user sales rely on intermediaries (exchanges, payment platforms). tether.wallet is like Tether installing its own branded faucet in users' homes for the first time.

As of March 2026, Tether's technology is used by over 570 million people worldwide, with tens of millions of new wallets added every quarter.

Prior to this, none of these 570 million people directly used Tether's own products. This meant that while Tether had a massive user base, it had no control over end-user behavior. If downstream exchanges or payment platforms encountered problems, Tether's network effect would be at risk of collapse. From a business perspective: Building a data flywheel and expanding business boundaries. Having products directly facing users means that Tether will accumulate first-party user behavior data for the first time. This data can support future product iterations, risk management optimization, and even provide insight reports to enterprise clients—a business value that cannot be achieved simply as back-end infrastructure. Meanwhile, this announcement is part of a series of aggressive moves by Tether in 2026: in March, it brought in KPMG to conduct its first comprehensive financial audit of its $185 billion USDT reserves, advancing USAT's compliance position in the US market; on April 9th, it released the QVAC AI development kit; and on April 14th, it launched tether.wallet. This entire series of moves points in the same direction: in an increasingly regulatory environment, while building institutional credibility, it locks in end-users through consumer products.

Regulatory Perspective: Seizing Strategic Advantage Before the GENIUS Act Takes Effect

Tether's 2026 strategy consists of two lines: KPMG audit to establish institutional credibility, USAT to establish a US regulatory position, and tether.wallet to directly push products to 570 million users.

The coordinated intention of these three is to complete the transformation from "gray infrastructure" to "compliant financial platform" before the formal implementation of the US GENIUS Act and other stablecoin regulatory frameworks. Once the regulatory framework is established, Tether, with its mature compliance system, ample audit records, and large direct user base, will have a significant advantage in terms of entry barriers.

III. The Profound Impact of tether.wallet on the Industry For the wallet market: A new player with a market capitalization of 570 million enters the fray For existing mainstream wallets such as MetaMask and Phantom, the emergence of tether.wallet signifies a fundamental change in the competitive landscape. Previously, these wallets' biggest competitive advantage was user habits and multi-asset support; however, Tether brings its twelve years of accumulated brand trust and the inherent use cases of USDT. More importantly, WDK, the platform behind tether.wallet, is already a proven open platform—Rumble Wallet is its successful commercial application. This means that Tether may continue to license WDK through white-label agreements, allowing more partners to build wallet products within the Tether ecosystem, forming a wallet ecosystem matrix where Tether controls the underlying protocol. For the stablecoin market: User stickiness shifts from platforms to issuers. Previously, users used USDT because they used Binance, OKX, or other platforms, not because they identified with the Tether brand. tether.wallet changes this dependency. Once users establish their [email protected] identity and usage habits within tether.wallet, some of USDT user stickiness will migrate from exchanges to Tether itself. This is a clear pressure signal for competitors like USDC—Circle currently lacks a substantial direct consumer wallet product.

On AI and the Machine Economy: A Human-Machine Shared Payment Infrastructure

Tether CEO Ardoino described his ambition for this wallet: "Tether aims to make digital infrastructure more accessible, serving tens of billions of future users." In the definition of WDK, "users" include not only humans, but also AI agents and autonomous machines. Tether explicitly positions WDK as "a financial infrastructure used by future humans, connected devices, and AI agents in high-speed transactions," designed to support trillions of self-custodial wallets.

This is a highly forward-looking strategic positioning. As the AI ​​agent economy evolves, autonomous payments between machines will become a real need, and self-custodial wallets with native stablecoin support and local AI capabilities will become the infrastructure for this scenario.

On the Industry Competitive Landscape: Blurring the Boundaries Between Issuers and Distributors

The deepest industry significance of the tether.wallet launch is that it breaks down the long-standing role division between "issuers and distributors" in the cryptocurrency industry.

Previously, Tether issued its own stablecoins, exchanges distributed them, and wallets handled them – each with its own role. Now, Tether chooses to directly reach users, effectively playing the triple role of issuer, distributor, and user interface provider. This will force other stablecoin issuers to rethink their distribution strategies and may also trigger vigilance and backlash from existing wallet platforms and exchanges. IV. Hidden Concerns Beyond the ideal narrative, any product has risk factors that require continuous monitoring. The tension between cloud backup and self-custody. tether.wallet allows users to back up their private keys to the cloud, a design that creates a potential tension between convenience and security. True self-custody requires complete user control of the keys, while cloud backup introduces third-party risks. It is currently unclear whether users can completely disable this feature. Centralization concerns regarding human-readable addresses. The [email protected] address format greatly improves the user experience, but it also means that this naming system is centrally operated by Tether. If Tether encounters problems at the corporate level, whether these addresses can continue to resolve normally remains an open question. The dual challenges of regulatory compliance: Launching a consumer wallet product means Tether will directly face stricter consumer protection regulations, a completely different compliance challenge from its previous role as merely a business-to-business infrastructure provider. KYC/AML obligations, user data protection regulations, etc., will all become new compliance costs. Conclusion: The launch of tether.wallet represents Tether's most important strategic transformation in twelve years. It signifies that an infrastructure company that has long operated behind the scenes has chosen to proactively step into the limelight during a crucial window of opportunity when regulatory scrutiny is becoming clearer and the competitive landscape is still uncertain. Regardless of future user growth figures, this move itself has sent a clear signal to the entire industry: the battlefield for stablecoins is extending from the protocol layer to the user end.

data.title: When people oppose AI, what exactly are they opposing?
data.description:
Shan Ouba, Golden Finance

In the spring of 2026, the gunshots in San Francisco's Pacific Heights community shattered a widely accepted assumption: opposition to artificial intelligence may no longer be limited to online condemnation and protests behind screens.

When the flames of Molotov cocktails pierced the night sky, when bullets shattered windows and doors, pointing directly at the residence of OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, we were forced to confront a harsh reality: in the shadow of the rapid iteration of technological revolution, humanity's fear of AI has been distorted into a weapon of harm.

I. 48 Hours, Two Terrifying Attacks

At 2:30 AM on April 11, 2026, in the quiet Pacific Heights neighborhood of San Francisco, a Molotov cocktail suddenly pierced the darkness, crashing heavily against the exterior wall of Sam Altman's residence. Flames briefly erupted, causing only minor damage to the exterior wall and no injuries, but it was enough to shake the entire tech world. Reportedly, three masked suspects quickly got into a black Honda Civic and fled the scene.

Just 48 hours later, panic struck again. At 1:15 a.m. on April 13, at the same location, a car slowly came to a stop, followed by a gunshot that shattered the silence, shattering windows. Fortunately, no one was injured in the attack. Police quickly intervened and arrested two suspects on the spot—25-year-old Amanda Tom and 23-year-old Muhamad Tarik Hussein—who were taken into custody for manslaughter. The FBI has officially launched an investigation, and the case is likely to escalate to terrorism-related charges. Subsequently, an anti-AI organization called "Pause AI" publicly claimed responsibility for both attacks. This is not a Hollywood suspense script, but a real violent incident that occurred to key figures in the tech world in the spring of 2026. II. From Peaceful Petitions to Violent Attacks: The Uncontrolled Escalation of the Anti-AI Movement If we rewind two years, the anti-AI movement remained within the realm of rational protests and peaceful petitions, characterized by restraint and expression of demands. In September 2024, the "AI Security Network" was officially established, its core activities focusing on policy lobbying, public petitions, and exposing industry irregularities, attempting to promote the implementation of AI regulation through legal channels. That same year, hundreds of anti-AI activists gathered outside OpenAI's headquarters, holding signs that read "Stop AI R&D," calmly and firmly conveying their demands to the government and businesses, calling for the establishment of a comprehensive AI regulatory system. The turning point came in 2025, when the anti-AI movement completely shifted, with moderate demands gradually being replaced by radical actions. In August of that year, over 500 protesters besieged OpenAI headquarters, causing chaos and resulting in the arrest of three people for disturbing public order. In November, Google's Oregon data center was deliberately vandalized by anti-AI activists, causing a two-hour power outage and direct economic losses of up to $2 million. This marked the first time anti-AI actions caused substantial property damage and signified the movement's radicalization. Entering 2026, the anti-AI movement accelerated further, with increasingly pronounced violent tendencies. In January, over 120,000 people signed a petition demanding the White House urgently suspend the GPT-5 training program, fearing its potential uncontrollable risks; in February, a large-scale sit-in demonstration erupted outside the Anthropic offices in New York, with 12 protesters arrested for refusing to disperse; in March, Nvidia's data center in California was vandalized again, with losses reaching $5 million, and two suspects arrested; in April, anti-AI activists targeted Sam Altman, a key figure in the AI ​​industry, using Molotov cocktails and bullets to push the protests to a violent peak. From petitions to deliberate vandalism, from property attacks to threats of violence, the escalating trajectory of the anti-AI movement bears a striking resemblance to the Luddite movement in history—in 1811, British textile workers, fearing machines would take their jobs, smashed textile equipment, using extreme methods to resist technological change. History doesn't simply repeat itself, but it always resonates with similar rhythms throughout the ages, providing a historical footnote to the current AI controversy.

III. Six Anxiety Behind Anti-AI Sentiment

To understand the out-of-control nature of the anti-AI movement, we must first understand the deep-seated fears behind it. These fears are not unfounded, but rather the real impact of technological change on human society, our living conditions, and our self-worth.

1. Job Displacement

This is the most direct and widespread fear. Data from 2025 shows that approximately 3.8 million people in the United States have already lost their jobs due to AI applications, including many white-collar positions once considered "stable and secure," such as customer service, translation, basic programming, and data analysis. These jobs are being replaced in large numbers by efficient AI systems.

McKinsey predicts that by 2030, approximately 45% of white-collar jobs globally will be directly impacted by AI, either being replaced or forced to transform. A former claims adjuster who worked at an insurance company for 15 years admitted in a November 2025 interview: “I did claims review for 15 years. Last year, out of 80 employees in my department, only 5 were left to handle ‘abnormal cases’ that AI couldn’t solve. The remaining 75 were all replaced by AI systems. We didn’t even have much buffer time.” For companies, adopting AI can reduce average costs by 60% and significantly increase shareholder returns. However, for ordinary employees, this means reduced income and a sharp increase in the risk of unemployment—the increase in efficiency has not translated into a corresponding increase in employment. This is the cruelest reality behind technological change. 2. Loss of Control Humans are inherently afraid of things that are “incomprehensible and uncontrollable.” No matter how complex traditional technologies are, humans can grasp their core principles and intervene, adjust, or even stop their operation at any time. However, the core of AI technology is "black box decision-making," whose operating logic and judgment criteria are difficult for humans to fully interpret. It may even develop autonomous decision-making capabilities during the learning process, exceeding human expectations and control. As UC Berkeley professor Stuart Russell stated, "The most terrifying misconception humans have about AI is not that it will rebel against humans, but that it will perfectly execute human errors—when we haven't considered all the risks, AI's 'absolute obedience' could have devastating consequences." 3. Increased Social Inequality Technological revolutions should bring universal benefits, but the distribution of AI dividends exhibits severe polarization. Estimates from 2025 indicate that of the total value created by AI, top AI company shareholders take approximately 45%, core AI researchers and engineers divide about 25%, while ordinary workers receive only about 15%, and many unemployed people replaced by AI don't even receive any dividends. A protester from the "Pause AI" organization angrily stated during a demonstration in February 2026: "AI company CEOs and shareholders are making a fortune, earning billions a year, while we lose our jobs and livelihoods because of AI. This isn't technological progress; it's blatant plunder." 4. The Disappearance of Human Uniqueness Art, writing, thinking, and creation—abilities once considered "exclusively human"—are now being broken down one by one by AI. Midjourney-generated artwork won international awards, GPT-5 passed top-level literature tests, and Devin can independently complete complex programming projects—when AI is better at creating, better at thinking, and even better at understanding human emotions and needs than humans, many people begin to wonder: what is the unique value of human existence?

5. Privacy Leaks

The popularization of AI technology has also brought about an unprecedented privacy crisis. Currently, 85% of major cities worldwide have deployed AI facial recognition systems, and 90% of internet user data is collected and analyzed by AI companies. AI's prediction accuracy for personal behavior has reached as high as 78%. Our travel routes, consumption habits, chat logs, and interests are all being quietly captured and interpreted by AI. Humans seem to be living in a transparent world without privacy, and our sense of security is constantly being eroded. 6. Doomsday Panic The media's penchant for exaggerating "AI out of control, human extinction" doomsday scenarios has exacerbated public panic. However, according to industry experts, the probability of these extreme scenarios occurring is extremely low: mass unemployment (85%, already occurred), deepfake abuse (75%, already occurred), AI weaponization (45%, expected in 5-10 years), complete AI out of control (5-15%, expected in 10-30 years), and human extinction (less than 1%). Unfortunately, the media often amplifies low-probability doomsday risks while ignoring the already occurring and widespread real-world harm.

IV. AI Controversies from the Perspective of Celebrities

Faced with the opportunities and risks brought by AI, celebrities from around the world have diverged in their opinions, forming three distinct camps, each holding firm to their positions and engaging in heated debates.

The Opposition: Firmly Call for a Halt, Be Vigilant Against Risks

This camp's voice is the loudest, with its core demand being to suspend AI research and development to prevent potential risks.

In December 2025, Elon Musk publicly stated, "AI is the greatest threat to human civilization, and the consequences would be unimaginable if it is not controlled in time." Apple co-founder Steve Wozniak also spoke out in January 2026, saying, "The development speed of AI has exceeded human control, and it must be paused immediately to reassess its risks." Yuval Noah Harari, author of *Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind*, warned in September 2025, "AI may completely end the human era and reshape the entire ecology and order of the Earth." Most thought-provoking is the statement by Jeffrey Hinton, known as the "Godfather of AI," who resolutely resigned from his position at Google in June 2025 and publicly confessed, "I regret participating in AI research and development. I realize that the technology I created may cause irreparable harm to humanity." Supporters: Embrace Change, Uphold Progress. Supporters firmly believe that AI... It is an inevitable trend of human progress, and fear should not be a reason to hinder technological development. Following the April 2026 attack, Sam Altman responded: "People's fear of AI is reasonable, I completely understand, but we cannot stop progressing because of fear. Historically, every technological revolution has been accompanied by skepticism and fear, but ultimately it has driven the prosperity and development of human society." Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg stated in January 2026: "AI is not a threat, but a tool to liberate human creativity. It can help us get rid of tedious repetitive work and focus on more valuable creations." Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang stated bluntly in December 2025: "AI is the core of the new industrial revolution. It will reshape the global industrial landscape and bring unprecedented development opportunities." AI expert Andrew Ng's view is more rational: "The AI ​​threat theory has been exaggerated by the media. In fact, AI will not replace humans; what will truly be replaced are those who are unwilling to learn and refuse to adapt to AI." a16z founder Marc Andreessen firmly stated: "AI..." It will save the world; it can solve major problems facing humanity such as climate change and disease prevention. Opposing AI is essentially opposing human progress. Neutral Faction: Balancing Innovation and Security Neutral factions attempt to find a balance between "promoting innovation" and "preventing risks," emphasizing the importance of regulation. Bill Gates stated, "AI brings tremendous development opportunities, but also harbors risks that cannot be ignored. The key is not 'whether or not to develop AI,' but how to guide its healthy development—we need a sound regulatory system that neither stifles innovation nor allows risks to spread." Google CEO Sundar Pichai holds a similar view: "The core of AI development is balance; we must maintain the vitality of innovation while upholding the bottom line of safety, ensuring that AI truly serves humanity." Apple CEO Tim Cook emphasized, "AI should adhere to a 'human-centered' approach; all technological research and development should revolve around human needs and well-being, rather than simply pursuing technological breakthroughs." V. A Mirror of History The anti-AI movement is not the first instance of resistance against new technologies in human history. Looking back, every major technological revolution has been accompanied by fierce opposition, and the patterns of history may offer insights into the current AI controversy. From 1811 to 1816, the famous Luddite movement erupted in Britain. Textile workers, fearing that machines would replace manual labor and take their jobs, smashed textile equipment and violently resisted technological change. Ultimately, the government suppressed the movement but also recognized the employment problems brought about by technological change, subsequently enacting a series of labor laws to protect workers' rights—technological development is unstoppable, but humanity can reduce its harm through institutional design. In the 1830s, with the rise of railway technology, anti-railway movements also broke out. People worried that trains would damage farmland, frighten livestock, and even believed that "high-speed movement would harm human health," leading to widespread opposition to railway construction. However, railways, with their efficiency and convenience, gradually became widespread, becoming a core driving force of the Industrial Revolution, and the anti-railway movement dissipated. From the 1950s to the present, the anti-nuclear movement has been ongoing, with opponents fearing the devastating consequences of nuclear leaks and nuclear war. This movement has achieved some success; global nuclear energy development is now strictly regulated, and nuclear safety systems are constantly improving. Similarly, since the 1990s, the anti-GMO movement has never ceased, with Europe even completely banning GMO crops, reflecting humanity's cautious attitude towards the risks of new technologies. Today, the anti-AI movement, which began in 2024, continues to ferment, and its outcome remains to be seen. But history has already shown a pattern: in the early stages of a new technology, it will inevitably encounter fierce opposition; in the middle stages, governments and society will intervene to regulate and standardize technological development; in the later stages, as the technology becomes more widespread and people gradually adapt, opposition will gradually weaken. However, it's worth noting that every technological revolution produces both "beneficiaries" and "victims." Some gain opportunities from technological progress, while others lose their livelihoods due to technological change. The core issue has never been "whether or not to develop technology," but rather "how to fairly distribute the benefits of technology" and how to protect those harmed by technology. This is precisely the core demand of the anti-AI movement and a challenge we must confront today. VI. Conclusion The bullet holes in Sam Altman's window are not a threat to one person, but a warning to the entire era. Those who threw Molotov cocktails weren't all crazy. Some were truck drivers who lost their jobs, graduates who couldn't see a future, and parents afraid their children would be replaced by AI. The fear is real, and the demands deserve to be heard, but violence is not the answer. The real answer lies not in the bullets, but in the system, in distribution, and in compensation for those "left behind." History has repeatedly proven that technology itself is neither good nor evil. When AI can do most things that humans can do, or even do them better, what is the value of humanity?

data.title: The "PayPal Mafia" of the AI ​​Era: From Interns to Billionaires
data.description:
Written by: Azuma

Yesterday, a feature interview with Hyperliquid founder Jeff Yan by Colossos magazine went viral online.

In the interview, Jeff Yan revealed a little-known story—during his junior year at Harvard, he participated in the first internship program of quantitative trading giant Hudson River Trading (HRT), with a total of 10 interns selected. Apart from Jeff Yan, who chose the cryptocurrency track, many of his fellow interns have now become prominent figures in the AI ​​field, including Alexandr Wang, head of Meta AI's business line; Jesse Zhang, founder and CEO of Decagon; and Scott Wu, founder and CEO of Cognition.

Jesse Zhang shared a group photo of the interns from that year

According to Scott Wu's supplementary disclosure, the HRT internship program was not the starting point of their friendship. Many of them had already met through the Olympiad competitions in high school (Jeff Yan, Scott Wu, and many others had won gold medals). That small circle also included many other prominent names, including, but not limited to, Johnny Ho, co-founder and CSO of Perplexity; Demi Guo, co-founder and CEO of Pika; Steven Hao, Alexandr Wang's former partner at Scale AI…

During the growth phase of Jeff Yan and Scott Wu, figures like Elon Musk and Peter Thiel represented the "PayPal Mafia"…

"Gangs" have already made their mark in the business world, and people are starting to look for the next similar special network. Jeff Yan and his circle of friends had discussed this topic before, and 19-year-old Alexandr Wang famously asked his friends, "Why can't it be us?" Ten years later, Alexandr Wang's bold statement seems to be coming true. Leveraging AI, this group of young people on the banks of the Hudson River are stirring up the times in their own way. Alexandr Wang: Zuckerberg's AI Brain Alexandr Wang is perhaps the most well-known person in this small circle. Born in Los Alamos, New Mexico in 1997. He is a descendant of Chinese immigrants; his parents were physicists at Los Alamos National Laboratory—the secret laboratory where the first atomic bomb of the United States was developed during World War II. Alexandr Wang has been passionate about mathematics and programming since childhood. He qualified for the Mathematical Olympiad in 2013, the US Physics Team in 2014, and reached the finals of the US Computer Olympiad in 2012 and 2013. In 2015, Alexandr Wang dropped out of MIT and founded Scale AI the following year, annotating data used to train AI in computer vision and audio transcription. Riding the wave of the AI ​​boom, Scale AI's valuation soared, reaching $7.3 billion in 2021, and Alexandr Wang, who owns 15% of the company, saw his net worth surpass $1 billion. In June 2025, Meta, which had clearly fallen behind in the AI ​​race, spent $14.3 billion to acquire a 49% stake in Scale AI. Zuckerberg's condition was that Alexandr Wang, the then 28-year-old key figure in Scale AI, must join Meta. Alexandr Wang subsequently joined Meta, taking charge of Meta's AI development team, Meta Superintelligence Labs (MSL). On the night of April 8th, Zuckerberg's gamble paid off when MSL officially released its first self-developed AI model, Muse Spark. Muse Spark is a native multimodal inference model that supports tool calls, visual chain thinking, and multi-agent orchestration. This is Meta's most powerful model to date. During training, MSL observed predictable scalability improvements in the model's pre-training, reinforcement learning, and inference phases during testing. Scott Wu: Olympiad Prodigy, Creator of a Billion-Dollar AI Industry

Scott Wu was born in 1997 in Louisiana to a Chinese immigrant background. Growing up, Scott Wu actively participated in programming and mathematics competitions, winning three gold medals in the International Olympiad in Informatics, including first place in 2014.

After graduating from high school, Scott Wu attended Harvard University, but dropped out after two years. While an undergraduate at Harvard, he participated in the 2016 International Collegiate Programming Contest (ICPC) as a member of the school team, which won a gold medal and achieved third place overall.

In 2019, Scott Wu co-founded the social platform Lunchclub as its Chief Technology Officer. In 2023, Scott Wu co-founded Cognition with friends Steven Hao and Walden Yan (both Olympic gold medalists), serving as CEO. In 2024, the Cognition team launched Devin, the world's first autonomous AI software engineer. This product can independently complete code writing, testing, and deployment, and supports the decomposition and collaboration of complex tasks, significantly outperforming GPT-4 in the SWE-bench benchmark test. In May of the same year, Cognition secured $175 million in funding led by Peter Thiel's Founders Fund, valuing the company at $2 billion post-money. In September 2025, Cognition raised another $400 million, pushing its valuation to $10.2 billion. As of early 2026, Cognition's annualized revenue had reached $400 million. Johnny Ho: Net worth $2.1 billion, once interested in acquiring TikTok and Chrome. Like Scott Wu, Johnny Ho, a Harvard graduate, won three gold medals at the International Olympiad in Informatics, including a perfect score in 2012. In August 2022, Johnny Ho co-founded Perplexity with Aravind Srinivas, Andy Konwinski, and Denis Yarats. Perplexity positions itself as an AI search engine company that provides conversational search engine services, displaying citation sources for answers and offering relevant question suggestions. In 2023, Perplexity already had 10 million monthly visits; by April 2024, its monthly active users had reached approximately 15 million. That same year, Perplexity embarked on a frenzied fundraising campaign, raising $5 million in its fourth funding round at the end of the year, reaching a valuation of $9 billion; in July 2025, Perplexity completed another $100 million funding round, raising its valuation to $18 billion. It's worth noting that Perplexity has made several ambitious "David and Goliath" acquisition proposals (with venture capital firms willing to fund them), including a takeover bid for TikTok in early 2025, proposing to merge Perplexity, TikTok's US operations, and a new capital partner into a new entity, and a proposal to Google in August 2025 to acquire its core product, the Chrome browser, for $34.5 billion. According to the latest data from Forbes, Perplexity is now valued at $20 billion, and Johnny Ho's personal wealth has reached $2.1 billion. Jesse Zhang: AI Startup Valued at $4.5 Billion in Three Years

Jesse Zhang was also born in 1997 and grew up in the San Francisco Bay Area. From high school onwards, Jesse Zhang was a typical "competition fanatic"—two-time winner of the American Mathematical Olympiad (MOP), a finalist in the Intel STS, and participated in MIT's RSI research project. After entering Harvard, Jesse Zhang completed four years of university coursework in just three years.

In 2018, Jesse Zhang and friends founded Lowkey, a gaming highlight sharing platform. The project received seed funding from Y Combinator and Series A funding from a16z.

In 2021, Lowkey was acquired by Niantic, the developer of Pokémon GO, for an undisclosed price. In 2023, Jesse Zhang and his partner Ashwin Sreenivas co-founded Decagon, focusing on automating enterprise customer service with AI agents to address the high labor costs and low efficiency of customer service centers. In June 2024, the newly established Decagon quickly secured $35 million in funding, including a $5 million seed round led by a16z and a $30 million Series A round led by Accel. Four months later, Decagon raised another $65 million in Series B funding. In June 2025, it raised $131 million in Series C funding, valuing the company at $1.5 billion. In January 2026, it raised $250 million in Series D funding, with its valuation soaring to $4.5 billion. Along with its rising valuation, Decagon's revenue generation capabilities also increased. By the end of 2025, the company's disclosed annual revenue had exceeded $30 million. Demi Guo: A Post-95s Pioneer in AI Video Generation from Hangzhou Demi Guo was born in Hangzhou, China in 1999. During her childhood, her family moved to Silicon Valley, USA. Demi Guo won a silver medal at the 2015 International Olympiad in Informatics. She graduated from Harvard University with a Bachelor of Science degree in Mathematics and a Master of Science degree in Computer Science. She then dropped out of her PhD program at Stanford University to focus on her entrepreneurial career in generative AI video content creation. In April 2023, Demi Guo co-founded Pika with Chenlin Meng, serving as CEO. Pika focuses on developing AI technology for video generation. Its core products include the Pika 1.0 and Pika 2.0 models, which support the generation of 3D animation, comics, cartoons, and movie-style content, offering features such as video extension, canvas expansion, and element replacement. In terms of funding, Pika completed a $20 million seed round before its official launch; subsequently, in November 2023, it completed a $35 million Series A funding round, led by Lightspeed Venture Partners; and in June 2024, Pika completed an $80 million Series B funding round at a valuation of $470 million, led by Spark Capital, with participation from Greycroft, Lightspeed Venture Partners, and actor Jared Leto, among others. Steven Hao: AI Tech Guru Worth Over $1 Billion

Steven Hao, a graduate of MIT's mathematics department, also won a gold medal at the International Olympiad in Informatics (IOI). He was Alexandr Wang's partner at Scale AI and has now joined Scott Wu's Cognition as CTO. Both companies have been described in detail previously, so they will not be repeated here.

Forbes data shows that Steven Hao, at only 30 years old, is now estimated to have a personal fortune of $1.3 billion.

Epilogue: We May Be Witnessing a New Legend I've considered giving this small circle a new name, similar to the "PayPal Mafia," such as the "Hudson River Mafia," or the broader "Olympia Mafia"... Although the historical context and story trajectory are completely different, they seem to share the same core spirit as the previous generation of the "PayPal Mafia"—behind the camaraderie of Olympia, what truly connects them is a shared pursuit of intellectual density, engineering efficiency, and system refactoring capabilities, and a profound prediction of where the future will begin. A new generation of entrepreneurs has stepped into the limelight. Before them lies the question of "How AI will reshape the world," a question far more challenging than the Olympiad itself. This is their battlefield, and their stage.

data.title: A Brief History of Web3 Airdrops: A Look at Twelve Iconic Anti-Airdrop Projects
data.description:
Once upon a time, airdrops in the cryptocurrency world were exhilarating "get-rich-quick myths." In the era of Uniswap, ENS, and Arbitrum, early users and project teams mutually benefited, and evangelists and builders shared the spoils, forming a brief but real "golden honeymoon."

However, turning the clock back to 2023-2026, the influx of massive capital, the extreme infighting of professional studios, and the insatiable appetite of project teams completely distorted the airdrop landscape.

"Interactive rewards" degenerated into a "cyber harvesting ground," and airdrops transformed from early-stage profits into a systemic reverse harvest.

Retail investors were redefined: free testers, low-cost liquidity providers, and a continuous stream of data producers.

In an environment where rules are opaque and expectations are repeatedly rewritten, what often ends up is not a reward, but rather being wiped out, diluted, or even directly eliminated. This article reviews 12 landmark "anti-airdrop" projects in airdrop history, examining how trust is gradually eroded. 1. Hop Protocol (HOP): Ushering in the "Sybil" Era. ● Anti-airdrop process: HOP, a star cross-chain bridge project, pioneered the chilling "Community Reporting Sybil" mechanism. The rules were extremely enticing: the reporter could take a share of the reported address. It felt like Shang Yang, who practiced collective punishment thousands of years ago, had traveled to Web3. ● Anti-airdrop characteristics: A grassroots mutual harm among the masses. The project team delegated the dirty work of verifying on-chain address associations to users, exploiting human greed to incite infighting within the community, and even directly uploading the list of whistleblowers to GitHub for the entire industry to "reuse." ● Profound Impact: After HOP, combating Sybil became "politically correct" for all token-issuing projects, and on-chain interaction transformed from "experiencing decentralized products" into an extremely cutthroat cat-and-mouse game. While combating Sybil has its necessity, completely shifting the responsibility of verification to the community, and even encouraging mutual harm, severely damaged the community ecosystem.

2. Blast: The Father of the Evil "Points System"

●The Anti-Penalty Process: Blast, with its top-tier Paradigm aura, abandoned the traditional interaction model, requiring users to lock up ETH or stablecoins in exchange for "points." The rules were changed frequently, resulting in huge profits for large holders and top NFT players, while ordinary users, after locking up their tokens for months, saw their token returns even fall short of the interest rates of risk-free financial products during the same period.

●Characteristics of the Anti-Penalty System: Ponzi scheme-like and blind box-like gambling.

●Characteristics of the Anti-Penalty System: Ponzi scheme-like and blind box-like gambling.

... Users are swept up in endless FOMO, becoming free ATMs for project teams' TVL data. ● Profound Impact: Since Blast, "points-based systems" have become the industry standard. The original intention of points systems was to encourage long-term user participation, but frequent rule adjustments and severe imbalances in rewards ultimately led to users losing trust in the projects. Web3 freeloaders became Web2 laborers, and the decentralized spirit that Web3 prided itself on died completely under the calculations of capital.

3. LayerZero (ZRO): The Tipping Point of Trust Collapse

●The Anti-Fraud Process: After 18 months of cross-chain interaction that caused users to burn through huge amounts of gas fees, the project team launched the strictest witch vetting in history on the eve of token issuance, even requiring users to "voluntarily confess" in exchange for a portion of their retained shares, otherwise they would be wiped out. A large number of real active users and small studios were wiped out.

●Anti-Fraud Characteristics: Extremely arrogant "presumption of guilt".

●Anti-Fraud Characteristics: Extremely arrogant "presumption of guilt".

The project team pocketed the exorbitant fees contributed by users, then turned around and treated them like thieves, guarding against and humiliating them. ● Profound Impact: LayerZero destroyed the grand narrative of "multi-chain interaction." While the manipulation of transaction volume by "Witchcraft" (a fraudulent platform) required censorship, the brutal implementation of the "presumption of guilt + confession mechanism" further accelerated the collapse of trust. From then on, Tencent (the company behind LayerZero) became infamous, and "anti-fraud" became a Damocles' sword hanging over the heads of all those who exploit loopholes. Retail investors finally understood: in the face of absolute interpretation, your efforts are worthless.

4. zkSync (ZK): The Complete End of the L2 Interactive Airdrop Era

●The Anti-Fraud Process: As one of the four former L2 kings, zkSync kept the community in suspense for several years. After absorbing hundreds of millions of dollars in gas fees, its airdrop rules revealed a shocking black box: the weight of transaction count and activity level was greatly reduced, and "funds retention over a specific period" was used as the core threshold. This resulted in a large number of long-term interactive users who had accompanied the project's growth receiving nothing, while internal insider trading and new accounts that had hastily deposited money received huge shares. ● Anti-fraud characteristics: Using "activity level" to scam Gas, then using "funds" to kick people out. ● Far-reaching impact: zkSync's extremely ugly practices made the entire market completely despair of L2 airdrops. Controlling witches and volume-boosting groups was necessary, but the opaque rules disheartened genuine early contributors. Subsequent L2 launches faced the predicament of "no one caring," and no retail investors were willing to become free on-chain laborers anymore.

5. Infinex: Public Sale Mechanism Collapse

●The Anti-Fraud Process: As a cross-chain DeFi aggregation platform endorsed by Synthetix founder Kain Warwick, Infinex was once seen as a representative of "orthodoxy" by the community. It induced users to invest significant funds and effort through Patron NFTs and a months-long points program. However, when the public sale began in January 2026, the community was met with an extremely high FDV valuation, an absurd "mandatory one-year lock-up," and a chaotic distribution logic.

6. Linea: The term "black slave" originated with Linea

●Anti-slave process: It took PUA art to an outrageous level: It launched the Galxe Odyssey quest, which lasted for two years and had an absurd number of phases. Users had to answer questions, cross-chain, swap, and mint illiquid garbage NFTs like black slaves, and finally they were forced to cooperate with extremely cumbersome KYC.

●Anti-slave characteristics: An indefinitely prolonged war of attrition.

Forever doing tasks, forever accumulating LXP points, forever being subjected to manipulation and psychological warfare, while the mainnet's token launch remains forever out of reach. ● Profound Impact: Linea turned "completing tasks to get airdrops" into a low-paying, mentally exhausting full-time job. Many users became so exhausted that they quit, marking the complete bankruptcy of the OAT (On-Chain Achievement Token) narrative.

7. Grass: DePIN's Free Generator

●Anti-Penetration Process: As a star in the DePIN track, it encourages users to contribute idle bandwidth by leaving their computers running. Countless people left their computers on 24 hours a day to farm points, even paying out of their own pockets to buy clean overseas IPs. As a result, when the token was issued, the project team kept the vast majority of the shares for themselves or allocated them to VCs. Retail investors worked hard to mine tokens for several months, but the money they got from selling them couldn't even cover the electricity bills and the cost of proxy IPs.

●Anti-Penetration Characteristics: Getting something for nothing.

●Anti-Penetration Characteristics: Getting something for nothing.

... Under the guise of Web3 development, it blatantly exploits the physical resources of Web2 users. ● Profound Impact: Grass's anti-exploitation efforts made the market realize that many so-called DePIN projects are essentially "free software," directly leading to a precipitous drop in retail investor participation in subsequent similar projects. 8. Monad: The L1 Airdrop Terminator ● Anti-exploitation Process: As a highly anticipated high-performance L1 project, Monad attracted long-term testnet interaction from the community. In October 2025, the MON airdrop was launched. Although 230,000 addresses were open for applications, the overall community allocation ratio was only about 3.3%. Many genuine testnet users were wiped out or received only meager shares due to strict Sybil vetting, while KOLs and some early associates received large allocations. ● Characteristics of the anti-freeze strategy: Extremely low allocation and strict vetting after high expectations. The project team attracted a large number of testnet users with a technical narrative, then distributed the tokens to KOLs. ● Far-reaching impact: The Monad incident further lowered the community's expectations for airdrops in new L1 projects. Although it was announced early on that the testnet wouldn't count, this wasn't stopped during the process, and none were received during the TGE, leaving genuine early contributors feeling betrayed. Subsequently, enthusiasm for similar high-performance L1 projects significantly declined, accelerating the shift in the L1 sector from "a hundred flowers blooming" to "cautious observation."

9. Babylon: The Incompatibility and Misapplication of the Bitcoin Ecosystem

●The Anti-Airdrop Process: Attempting to forcibly transplant Ethereum's staking mechanism to the Bitcoin network. During the mainnet event, due to the capacity limitations of the BTC chain and extremely high network congestion, many retail investors paid exorbitant miner fees but still failed to stake, directly resulting in real financial losses. Those users who were lucky enough to successfully stake found, after locking up their tokens for six months, that the airdrop returns were far less than those from trading on exchanges or buying financial products.

● **Characteristics of Anti-Fraud:** Extremely high trial-and-error costs. Forcibly creating FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) sentiment on the BTC chain, which does not support smart contracts, ultimately leads to retail investors being mercilessly burned by exorbitant gas fees. ● **Far-reaching Impact:** It poured extremely cold water on the overheated BTC L2 market. It proved with painful lessons that simply replicating Ethereum's PUA (Personal Aid) model is fundamentally unworkable in the Bitcoin ecosystem. This severely eroded the trust and patience of veteran Bitcoin players in this emerging ecosystem.

10. Backpack: The Backlash of Frenzied Volume Boosting and the Trust Crisis of "Chinese Ponzi Schemes"

●The Anti-Fraud Process: Backpack, which raised $37 million, launched a "trading volume = points" activity, and manipulated the community for two years. On the eve of TGE, a sudden crackdown on strict KYC and the "one device, one IP" black box witch hunt resulted in the wiping of a large number of accounts. The survivors suffered equally badly: a large investor boosted $15 billion in volume, spending $300,000 in transaction fees, only to receive $150,000 in tokens (a net loss of 50%). Users' real money was directly converted into the project team's profit. ●Characteristics of "Anti-Fraud": A simple and brutal form of "reverse pump-and-dump." While wash trading does require strict scrutiny, only reviewing it after token issuance is blatantly using the guise of airdrops to earn transaction fees. Furthermore, the token BP plummeted 68% in its first week, with users being quietly drained by endless wash trading. ●Long-Term Impact: The image of Chinese entrepreneurs has been completely tarnished. The Chinese region became the hardest hit, and the stereotype of "Chinese-led projects = anti-fraud" is deeply ingrained in the community, leading to an unprecedented crisis of trust for subsequent Chinese-led Web3 projects during their initial launch.

11. EdgeX: The Decline of Perp DEX

●The Anti-Airdrop Process: After the L2 crash, Perp DEX, which required real money to pay transaction fees, was once regarded by retail investors as the last haven for airdrops. Although Lighter set a good precedent, by the time edgeX TGE: old users spent hundreds of thousands of dollars in transaction fees to get airdrops worth less than a thousand dollars, while more than 80 new "insider trading" addresses with no interaction records raked in nearly $100 million in shares. Subsequently, on-chain investigators confirmed its connection to black market activities by market makers, and the official account immediately shut down comments and disappeared, leaving behind a mess. ● Characteristics of Anti-Fraud: Openly rigging insider trading, retail investors acting as data cows, project teams don't even bother to pretend. ● Far-reaching Impact: The EdgeX fiasco completely shattered the narrative of Perp DEX's volume manipulation, turning endorsements from top institutions into a euphemism for sophisticated exploitation. Retail investors were utterly desperate, and smart money accelerated its return to CEXs or native L1 exchanges.

12. Genius: The Last Straw That Broke the Loose for the Loose Group

●The Anti-Loose Process: Genius was seen as the last hope, but after the community frantically boosted trading volume, TGE received a reversal gift: immediately claiming the airdrop within 7 days would automatically burn 70% of the tokens, with a maximum of 30%; or choosing to lock up for one year to receive the full amount. Under the overwhelming public pressure, the project team then urgently launched a "refund" option - within 48 hours of TGE, users could choose to burn 100% of the airdrop amount in exchange for a refund of the fees charged by Genius.

●Characteristics of Anti-Profiteering: Users invest real money based on a premium of trust, only to be told at the last minute, "Either take a small amount and leave, or spend another year with the project team."

●Profound Impact: Genius's questionable actions completely demystified the "top-tier platform narrative." It was described by the community as "the last straw that broke the camel's back for profiteering enthusiasts."

●Characteristics of Anti-Profiteering: Users invest real money based on a premium of trust, only to be told at the last moment, "Either take a small amount and leave, or spend another year with the project team."

●Profound Impact: Genius's questionable actions completely demystified the "top-tier platform narrative." It was called "the last straw that broke the camel's back for profiteering

Conclusion: A Decisive Action to Get to the Root of the Problem

From HOP's witch hunt list to Blast's nested points system, and then to LayerZero's self-surrender and massacre... these twelve project teams have collectively written an absurd and cruel history of blood and tears for retail investors in the cryptocurrency world.

But the truth may be even more cruel: this is not only a premeditated harvest, but also a collective karma of speculation and greed.

But the truth may be even more cruel: this is not only a premeditated harvest, but also a collective karma of speculation and greed.

For a long time, the airdrop community only cared about "whether or not to issue tokens and how to distribute them," ignoring whether the products had genuine product-to-market value (PMF) or could generate sustainable revenue. Project teams precisely exploited this greed – you were after the airdrop, they were after your principal and transaction fees. Now, the airdrop bubble has burst, and countless people have been severely burned. While this is undoubtedly tragic, it's also a necessary, albeit painful, cleansing. The market has finally been forced to return to common sense: traffic attracted by airdrop expectations is ultimately a mirage; only products with genuine PMF are worth investing time and money in. This is the end of airdrops and the rebirth of Web3. Those projects that started with manipulation and opaque practices will ultimately be eliminated by users' votes with their feet; while those projects truly willing to build with the community and return to the essence of value will win more precious trust from the ruins. For those who enjoy admiring and embracing certain things, this was a painful lesson, but also a sobering turning point.

data.title: Solana, please match your speed.
data.description:
Author: Jito Labs CEO/Co-founder Lucas Bruder

Original Title: Solana's Napster Era Is Over

Compiled and edited by: BitpushNews

Solana is a masterpiece. But what's built on top of it doesn't deserve that title.

I'm not saying this lightly. My team has been working on Solana's infrastructure layer for four years. We've witnessed every stage of this chain's growth: crashes, recoveries, explosive growth in activity, and the maturation of the validator network. We've watched transaction volumes grow from a few thousand to millions, to numbers that make traditional exchanges uneasy. I believe in this chain more than anyone else.

But infrastructure isn't what people experience. People experience the applications.

Now, almost nothing on Solana is built for users who are already there and deserve a better experience, let alone the millions coming soon. How did we get here? This mirrors the trajectory of the internet. The first large-scale applications of the internet were pirated MP3s and pornography. I was six when Napster launched in 1999, and within two years it had 80 million users sharing pirated Metallica albums. Early internet engineers thought they were building the future of communication, but the first wave of users emerged for the most "degenerate" possibilities. This wasn't a failure of the internet, but rather proof that the "pipeline" was working. It just took a few more years before Amazon, Google, or the iPhone emerged on the same infrastructure. Solana has been in its Napster era. Its infrastructure is indeed world-class. The entire developer ecosystem revolves around "shortest-handling with the latest tokens": more token issuance platforms, more dashboards tracking memecoin price movements. All development targets the same set of wallets, and all optimizations are designed to help people get in and out as quickly as possible, not to help them trade better. Supporters have consistently argued that memecoin was crucial for developing and stress-testing Solana's engineering capabilities. They are right, but that argument belongs to the past. Dogecoin brought us here, but it's not the destination. Whether the ecosystem is ready or not, the Napster era is ending.

DEX vs. CEX Spot Volume Ratio (Source: Blockworks Analytics)

The world's largest fintech companies are racing to bring users on-chain.

Coinbase acquired Vector.fun last year as part of its massive acquisition spree, and now any Coinbase user can trade virtually any Solana token directly from the Coinbase App. Solana's DEX trading volume surpasses $1 trillion by 2025.

Robinhood is building its own blockchain to support tokenized stocks and perpetual futures, and you can already buy Solana tokens directly in your Robinhood wallet. These companies, with hundreds of millions of users combined, are no longer building standalone crypto products but weaving on-chain trading into the same experience people have when buying stocks and ETFs. The walls between CEXs (centralized exchanges) and DEXs (decentralized exchanges) are crumbling. And they chose Solana's infrastructure because it's the best. The next wave of 100 million users isn't a myth; they already have Coinbase and Robinhood installed on their phones. The question is, what will they find when they arrive? Currently, using many Solana apps feels like walking into a flea market. It's noisy everywhere, there are no labels, and you're unsure what's real and what's fake. There might be some good deals, but the environment doesn't give you any reason to trust it. In contrast, Robinhood and Coinbase have clean, uncluttered interfaces and intuitive controls; the whole experience makes you feel like someone anticipated your needs before you even had to. That's the difference. And that's exactly how the next wave of users will measure Solana.

Look at what Hyperliquid is doing: less emphasis on memes and a growing focus on real-world assets. Daily trading volume for oil perpetual contracts reaches $1.7 billion; S&P 500 contracts surpassed $100 million on their first day. The protocol generates over $600 million in annualized fees, and its token HYPE is up 50% this year, while most tokens have performed poorly. They've proven that when you build a serious trading experience, real trading volume follows. And they're doing it on their own L1, without Solana's advantage. We don't have technical problems; we have experience problems. Imagine what it's possible when someone develops at this level on top of the fastest infrastructure in the cryptocurrency space. The three dimensions of the experience gap: The first dimension is trust. People are used to apps that are intuitive and easy to use from the moment they open. Robinhood, Cash App, Apple Pay. You don't need to read manuals or decode interfaces. Everything works as expected, and when something goes wrong, the app tells you why in plain language. That's the baseline. Now think about what happens when these users first enter a Solana app. The experience immediately feels untrustworthy—not because of smart contracts or the escrow model, but because the interface doesn't look designed for them. The colors and the three vertical trend bars are practically a slot machine. No one explained what happened or why. They closed the tab within 30 seconds and went back to where they were. They weren't churned; they were never given a reason to stay. Trust is the feeling you get within the first three seconds of using a product—that feeling that "the developer respects your time and intelligence." Most products in this ecosystem are designed for people who already understand cryptocurrency. This works when all users are crypto natives; but it doesn't work when fintech companies are about to reach millions of people who don't even know what a mnemonic phrase is. The second dimension is craftsmanship. Craftsmanship is the result that developers truly "care" about. It's not just about speed of delivery, but about every detail from when you click to trade to settlement. How does the interface respond under network pressure? Does the loading status convey progress or just spin? Does the confirmation screen tell you the core information or bury it? Craftsmanship is a feeling: every interaction, every extreme situation, is anticipated by someone who understands your intentions. The Solana ecosystem has always moved too fast, so most teams have chosen to optimize delivery speed rather than this dedication. In the growth phase, that was the right choice; but the next phase requires different standards. The third dimension is durability. Most projects in the crypto space are optimized for the current cycle. Teams launch quickly, grab attention, follow the narrative, and leave when the narrative shifts. The entire ecosystem operates on a "hype and abandonment" rhythm. Users can feel this. They've been hurt too many times; they've developed an instinct. They can distinguish what's built for long-term existence from what's for short-term arbitrage. The truly important applications on Solana are those projects that teams see as a decade-long opportunity, not a cyclical trade. They are the applications that survive three, five, or ten years later, through bear markets and shifting landscapes. Traders know the difference. Nobody's going to spend time configuring a tool they think will disappear next year. In conclusion, Solana is at a turning point, and most in the ecosystem haven't realized it yet. The debate about infrastructure has settled. Solana is fast, cheap, and reliable. That's now the ticket, not a differentiating advantage. The next phase of competition isn't between chains, but between the applications built on top of them. And right now, the application layer doesn't match the ambition of its underlying infrastructure. The Napster era proved the chain viable. Millions of transactions daily, billions of dollars in volume, it withstood real pressure. That argument has won. What's next is harder: it requires building the next wave of applications that 100 million users truly want to use. The experiences that Coinbase and Robinhood are willing to proudly showcase to their customers. These experiences should make Hyperliquid's success on its own L1 look like a glimpse into the infinite possibilities of Solana, rather than a accusation that we failed to build anything good. The chain has grown. Now, it's time for everything on top of it to catch up.

data.title: Has the war with Iran ended?
data.description:
Author: AndyO；Source: X，@andyz8818576155

The Iran war has not officially ended, but the nature of the war has changed from the moment US warships entered the fray and effectively blockaded the waterways related to the Hormuz.

It is shifting from "military strikes" to "forcing Iran to surrender."

The market direction will be determined by whether Iran will be forced to accept an extremely undignified agreement after losing the leverage of the Hormuz.

The next factor to determine the market direction is:

II. The driving force for peace is strengthening.

Although a final agreement is unlikely to be signed directly in the short term, the underlying forces driving the situation toward "limited peace" are indeed rising.

1. The economic pressure within Iran is no longer just ordinary difficulty, but is close to being out of control.

The strongest driving force for peace now is not the US-led TACO, nor European pressure, but rather Iran itself is on the verge of collapse.

The Central Bank of Iran has warned that if the fighting continues, inflation will surge to 180%, unemployment will increase by 2 million, and the economy will regress by more than a decade.

For any regime, once the internal economic order begins to collapse, so-called toughness will ultimately have to give way to maintaining stability.

2. ... It's clear that the US leadership doesn't want to prolong the war indefinitely. Trump has repeatedly signaled his desire for an end to the war, saying that Iran has reached an agreement with him and that many issues are "almost settled." While not all of this is entirely credible, it at least indicates one thing: the White House now prefers a "publiceable agreement" rather than a long-term quagmire that will consume oil prices, inflation, and votes. 3. Capital markets have already reacted. The S&P 500 has erased its losses since the Iran war, and the Nasdaq and Bitcoin are both strengthening. This reflects capital betting that the negotiations will likely eventually return to the negotiating table. The logic of the capital market is very realistic—as long as things don't spiral out of control, as long as the Hormuz agreement isn't permanently paralyzed, and as long as there's still a window for negotiations between the US and Iran, funds will first adjust for the "worst-case scenario." Currently, there's a clear divergence between oil and the stock market: oil prices are pricing in real risks, while the stock market is pricing in optimistic expectations for future peace talks. III. Is this "True Peace"? The real underlying contradictions in the Iranian peace talks remain unresolved. 1. Iranian negotiators likely don't have the final say. Vance has already stated it bluntly: the Iranian delegation at the Pakistan talks simply doesn't have the ability to make the final decision. The real decision-makers are at a higher level, perhaps even the Supreme Leader himself. This means that even if the atmosphere at the negotiating table is good, it doesn't guarantee an agreement will be implemented. Because what Iran is giving up isn't some minor detail, but the nuclear issue, a national security strategy, and the legitimacy of its regime. Such matters cannot be decided internally in just a few days. 2. The differences between the US and Iran on the nuclear issue remain structural. What the US wants is: a long-term halt to the enrichment program (30 years), the removal of stockpiles, and ideally, the complete crippling of Iran's future nuclear capabilities. What Iran wants is: to retain some leeway, accepting only a shorter window of opportunity (5 years), and replacing a complete surrender with dilution. This is not a typical disagreement where "details can be discussed later." One wants you to surrender your weapons, the other only wants to surrender a smaller amount of ammunition. This structural contradiction cannot be resolved with just a few rounds of pleasantries. 3. The specific arrangements for the next round of talks haven't even been finalized yet. Currently, the US, Pakistan, and Turkey are all pushing for the next round of face-to-face talks. Note that it's "pushing for the next round," not "already preparing to sign." This indicates that things are still in the diplomatic rhetoric and tactical probing stage, far from being truly finalized. 4. The real impact of the blockade may be more cautious than political pronouncements. Trump can talk about how many ships passed yesterday to downplay the impact. But the market sees something else entirely: Will ship owners dare to continue sending ships? Will insurance companies dare to continue underwriting? Will shipping costs skyrocket? Will oil prices rise further due to continued expectations of Iranian supply disruptions? If the blockade changes from a verbal threat to a sustained physical reality, then a second wave of oil price and inflation shocks will not be a story, but a reality. This is why Federal Reserve officials are already warning that once oil prices continue to rise above $90, the risks of inflation spillover and deteriorating consumer sentiment will increase. 5. Agent networks remain the biggest tail risk. Hezbollah has openly defied the US, and there are reports of a possible attack on the Bab el-Mandeb Strait from the Houthi perspective. This situation is more dangerous than many people realize. This is because the Strait of Hormuz is a global energy lifeline, and the Bab el-Mandeb Strait is a global shipping chokepoint. If both of these points are compromised simultaneously, the impact will not only be on oil, but on the entire global trade system. If an attack also occurs on the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, the current market optimism based on the expectation that "the situation will gradually ease" will be instantly shattered. IV. Conclusion From the very first day of the war, my fundamental judgment has remained unchanged: In terms of conventional warfare capabilities, Iran does not possess the ability to directly confront the US and Israel to the end. Iran's true fighting capability lies not in conventional warfare, but in "unrestricted warfare": utilizing proxy networks, the Taiwan Strait, and global energy prices to blackmail its adversaries. However, now that the US has decided to deploy warships and take direct control, it's tantamount to dismantling Iran's strongest geopolitical leverage. Therefore, I believe that the strategic phase of the Iran war is already nearing its end. The uncertainty now isn't whether Iran can turn the tide, but whether the hardliners of the Iranian Revolutionary Guard are willing to concede gracefully. If they don't concede, there's a risk of accidental clashes, escalation of proxy wars, and renewed localized conflict. If Iran concedes defeat, the regime will face enormous internal turmoil, as this would be tantamount to being forced to accept a major concession on the nuclear issue while being controlled by the United States in the Strait of Hormuz. This would have a significant impact on Iranian internal politics and could potentially lead to regime change. V. What does this mean for the market? Short-term outlook is slightly positive, but long-term volatility remains a risk. The reason for the short-term positive outlook is simple—the market will continue to trade on the "war nearing its end, increased probability of peace talks, and restored risk appetite." As long as there is no escalation of both the Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb Strait incidents, short-term sentiment will remain supported. However, the long-term outlook may not be good. Because if Iran, a crucial pivot in the Middle East, is crippled, the geopolitical landscape will undergo profound changes, rewriting the subsequent energy, allies, and regional balance. Is Iran truly willing to concede and relinquish its nuclear capabilities? Sixth, Investment Strategy. My strategy remains unchanged: For A-shares, short-term position allocation will continue to be determined by the CSI 1000 index. As long as the price remains near and holds above the 20-day moving average, the short-term bias remains bullish. In terms of direction, continue to consider short-term opportunities in two types of stocks: The first category is undervalued stocks with projected earnings growth. The second category is stocks with clear price increase logic and the most direct profit elasticity. Because in the current environment, the most certainty is never in storytelling, but in performance. Especially for price increase themes, once the price transmission begins to materialize, it's the direction most easily reinforced by the market. The strategy is simple: hold long positions above the 20-day moving average, and exit if it falls below. This short-term strategy has remained unchanged from last week to today. Those who like short-term trading should just follow the discipline; don't chase hot topics one minute and get scared off by news the next. Regarding long-term investment, my view remains unchanged: **Now is not the best time to buy.** The truly comfortable long-term buying point is often not when the market is still debating whether there will be a war or negotiations, but rather after the risks have been more fully priced in. Therefore, be patient and don't rush. In conclusion: **The current reality is not that "peace has arrived," but rather that "the willingness to negotiate is increasing, but overall, pressure is being applied while negotiations continue."** The US is using the Strait of Hormuz for maximum pressure, while Iran is using proxies and delaying tactics to counter blackmail. The market has already priced in optimistic expectations but hasn't left enough safety margin for "shipping disruptions, a second wave of oil price shocks, and a breakdown in nuclear negotiations." Furthermore, if Iran were to surrender, would it truly be a good thing for China (considering the loss of its geopolitical allies in South America and the Middle East)? Therefore, in terms of trading strategy, short-term traders can continue to buy based on moving averages, while long-term traders should remain cautious. Don't overestimate Iran's ability to turn the tide, and don't underestimate the long-tail risk of hardliners making a last-ditch effort. This is the most crucial point to understand in today's market.

April 14, 2026, according to Reuters: Federal Reserve Chairman nominee Kevin Warsh listed dozens of future-oriented assets in his latest financial disclosure filing, including Elon Musk's SpaceX and the prediction platform Polymarket. The filing shows that Warsh has made dozens of seemingly small investments in a range of emerging and almost science fiction-like projects.

Warsh's major holdings bring his assets to over $100 million, including two stakes in Juggernaut Fund LP worth over $50 million.

Among his dozens of other holdings, Warsh's traditional central banker image has shifted. These holdings are listed under a company called DCM Investments 10 LLC, with a market value of less than $500,000. In these holdings, Warsh's image gradually transforms into a vision of the future: digital AI avatars offering advice, AI-driven art, new herpes vaccines, long-acting reversible male contraceptives, and decentralized derivatives trading.

This article reviews Warsh's investment empire. I.
